channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: White House Considers Vetting A.I. Models Before They Are Released
item.description: The Trump administration, which took a noninterventionist approach to artificial intelligence, is now discussing imposing oversight on A.I. models before they are made publicly available.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle, Julian E. Barnes, Sheera Frenkel and Dustin Volz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration’s shift in policy began after Anthropic introduced a powerful artificial intelligence model called Mythos.

item.title: Oil Jumps as Violence Flares After Trump’s Offer to Help Ships Through Strait
item.description: Oil prices fell and futures on the S&P 500 rose slightly on Sunday as investors reacted to continued uncertainty about the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Charles Krupa/Associated Press

item.title: U.S. Warns China Over Iranian Oil as Sanctions Fight Intensifies
item.description: China told its independent refineries to disregard U.S. sanctions over their purchases of Iranian crude.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The warning came from Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who is expected to participate in high-stakes meetings this month between the United States and China.

item.title: Met Gala 2026 Red Carpet Photos: All the Looks
item.description: See the arrivals on the cobblestone carpet.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk, Nina Westervelt and Amir Hamja
item.media:description: Emma Chamberlain in Mugler.

item.title: The U.S. Army’s ‘Big Experiment’ in the Arctic Cold
item.description: How would soldiers from places like Florida, Texas and Georgia fight and persevere in temperatures as low as minus 40 degrees?
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Kenny Holston
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Judge Questions Why Correspondents’ Dinner Shooting Suspect Was Placed on Suicide Watch
item.description: A federal magistrate ordered that the D.C. Department of Corrections explain why it had placed Cole Tomas Allen in especially restrictive conditions.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Law enforcement responding to the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner on April 25.

item.title: Supreme Court Temporarily Restores Access to Abortion Pill by Mail
item.description: A lower-court ruling had reinstated a Food and Drug Administration requirement that patients visit a health care provider in person to obtain mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court has for now restored nationwide access to a widely used abortion pill.

item.title: Mifepristone, the Abortion Pill, Returns to the Supreme Court. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The Louisiana lawsuit, which seeks to block telehealth prescribing and mailing of the abortion pill mifepristone, has major implications for abortion access nationwide.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: After an appeals court ruling Friday, some telehealth abortion services started shipping only misoprostol, typically the second drug used after mifepristone in the medication abortion regimen. Services resumed sending both medications on Monday after the Supreme Court temporarily restored access.

item.title: New York Times Wins 3 Pulitzer Prizes
item.description: The Washington Post won the award for public service, considered the most prestigious of the Pulitzers, for its coverage of the Trump administration’s overhaul of federal agencies.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: A photo that was part of the Pulitzer Prize-winning entry by Saher Alghorra, a contributor to The New York Times, in the breaking news photo category. It showed a child wounded in Gaza City being transferred to a hospital in April last year.

item.title: Pulitzer Prizes: 2026 Winners List
item.description: Here is the full list of winners and finalists.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: This image, showing displaced Palestinians walking to Gaza City in October 2025, was part of the entry by Saher Alghorra, a contributor to The New York Times who won for breaking news photography.

item.title: A Portfolio of Work From The Times’s Pulitzer Prize-Winning Photographer
item.description: The photographer Saher Alghorra reported on the Gaza conflict while enduring the dire conditions his images portrayed.
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Displaced Palestinians began returning to the north of Gaza after the Israeli military announced that a cease-fire was in effect.

item.title: Mark Carney Pulls Canada Closer to Europe as Both Struggle With Trump
item.description: At a summit of European leaders, the Canadian prime minister was a special guest offering deals and friendship to jittery allies.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Anthony Pizzoferrato/Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving for a meeting of the European Political Community on Monday in Yerevan, Armenia.

item.title: Europeans Prepare for a More Dangerous World in a Time of Economic Upheaval
item.description: A stalwart on defense spending, Poland is training its citizens in civil defense while balancing their jobs and families.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.media:credit: Maciek Nabrdalik for The New York Times
item.media:description: Polish civilians gathered at the headquarters of the 13th Silesian Territorial Defense Brigade in southern Poland for emergency preparedness training.

item.title: Germans Are Not Panicking Over Trump’s Troop Threats
item.description: Top leaders believe losing 5,000 U.S. troops will not hurt German security. But analysts fear an economic hit, especially in military communities.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Angelika Warmuth/Reuters
item.media:description: Soldiers at a U.S. training facility in Hohenfels, southern Germany, last month.

item.title: More Than 150 Wind Projects Stall as Pentagon Delays Reviews
item.description: The delays, which companies say have worsened significantly in recent weeks, are the latest step in the Trump administration’s efforts to block wind power.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Wind turbine components on a lot in Iowa last summer.

item.title: Do Spinning Wind Turbines Really Mess With Radar Systems?
item.description: As Pentagon officials delay approval of wind farms, scientific studies show that they can distort radar systems, but the problem is manageable, researchers say.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Scientists, engineers and federal regulators have worked together for more than 15 years to manage radar interference by wind turbines.

item.title: Home on the Range No More: Trump Wants Bison Gone
item.description: The Trump administration is evicting bison herds from federal grasslands in Montana, siding with ranchers and Republican leaders over environmentalists and tribal leaders.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and John Stember
item.media:description: American Prairie has identified the northern grasslands of Montana as the only place left in the United States to restore an entire prairie ecosystem.

item.title: Trump Administration Orders Rapid End to Hunting Regulations on Federal Lands
item.description: Internal Interior Department documents show parks are imposing sweeping changes like expanding areas where firearms can by used and allowing game to be cleaned in restrooms.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve (La)
item.media:credit: Alamy
item.media:description: Curecanti National Recreation Area in Colorado, where a prohibition on discharging firearms around trails is one of dozens of hunting regulations that will be taken off the books.

item.title: Chasing Stardom in Korea Nearly Destroyed Ejae. Then Came ‘KPop Demon Hunters.’
item.description: Ejae, the movie’s breakout star, was the face of K-pop in 2025. Facing questions of art, national belonging and authenticity, she wants to be much more.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ejae in the Hannam neighborhood of Seoul in April. She spent about a decade training to be a K-pop star in the city.

item.title: The Return for These Investors Isn’t Money, It’s More Affordable Housing
item.description: Local governments are trying to create housing that is permanently affordable by investing directly in construction. They are rewriting how housing programs have traditionally operated.
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty
item.media:credit: Julie Holder for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Bedsole of Invest Chattanooga has helped seed housing funds in several cities.

item.title: A 22,000-Home Community Is Being Built in a 5,000-Person Town
item.description: Chatham Park, a master-planned community in Pittsboro, N.C., is expected to expand the town to 60,000 people when completed.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Abrams
item.media:credit: Sebastian Siadecki for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chatham County Historic Courthouse in downtown Pittsboro, N.C.

item.title: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni Settle ‘It Ends With Us’ Lawsuit
item.description: Ms. Lively had accused Mr. Baldoni of waging a smear campaign against her after she complained of harassment when making a film together. He had denied her accusation.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Blake Lively and Justin Baldoni starred in the movie “It Ends With Us.”

item.title: Rudy Giuliani Hospitalized in Florida in ‘Critical Condition’
item.description: A spokesman for the former mayor of New York City said Mr. Giuliani was now breathing on his own.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rudy Giuliani, the former mayor of New York, is critically ill in a Florida hospital.

item.title: Modi’s B.J.P. Wins West Bengal State Election for the First Time
item.description: The party of Prime Minister Narendra Modi took control of West Bengal for the first time and made gains around the country. Its defeated opponents accused it of cheating.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Hari Kumar and Pragati K.B.
item.media:credit: Sajjad Hussain/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India celebrating at the Bharatiya Janata Party headquarters in New Delhi on Monday after the party’s election victory in the state of West Bengal.

item.title: Is Loving Food the Secret to Eating Less of It?
item.description: The case for ditching self-denial and embracing enjoyment.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells
item.media:credit: Ricardo Tomás

item.title: Streisand, LuPone, Holliday, Menzel: Broadway’s Biggest Voices
item.description: Lea Michele, Christine Ebersole, Jennifer Hudson, Kristin Chenoweth, Adam Lambert and 10 others reflect on their favorite belters.

item.title: Speak, Yuppie
item.description: The young urban professionals of the 1980s changed our cities, our politics and our meritocratic work lives.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Gottlieb
item.category: Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP

item.title: American Commentator Who Worked for Russian TV Challenges Biden-era Charges
item.description: The Biden administration called Dimitri Simes, a former Trump adviser, a Russian propagandist. He says the charges against him violate free speech.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Evgenia Novozhenina/Reuters
item.media:description: Dimitri K. Simes, now living permanently in Moscow, said he believed that the Biden administration’s antipathy had its roots in the allegations that he had been involved in efforts by Russia to contact the Trump campaign.

item.title: What to Know About the Suspected Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: Three passengers of the MV Hondius died after showing symptoms of the rare disease. Health authorities confirmed one case and are investigating five others.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young and Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.
